text: Caffeine has a specific 'half-life' — the time it takes for your body to eliminate 50% of the caffeine you consumed. Based on sleep research, what is caffeine's average half-life in the human body?

choices.hint: This is how long it takes for caffeine to reach peak concentration, not its half-life.
Much too fast. If this were true, an afternoon coffee would clear by dinner.
Still too short. Many people report poor sleep from coffee 8+ hours earlier.
Getting warmer, but caffeine persists longer than most people think.
This is closer to the time it takes to mostly clear caffeine, but it's not the half-life.
By this point most caffeine is gone, but the half-life is shorter.
This would mean a morning coffee is half-present at midnight. Too long for the half-life.
Nearly a full day — far too long for a single half-life.

choices.text: It varies so much it can't be measured
choices.hint: While genetics do affect clearance speed, the average half-life is well-established.

correctExplanation: Caffeine's half-life is 5–7 hours. That means if you drink a cup of coffee at 7:30 PM, by 1:30 AM you still have HALF that caffeine circulating in your brain. A coffee after dinner is the biochemical equivalent of getting into bed and having someone drip half a cup of coffee into your IV line.

text: The human body has an internal clock that runs on a cycle slightly different from 24 hours. Research has found the average human circadian rhythm runs on a cycle of approximately how long?

choices.hint: Close, but if it were exactly 24 hours, we wouldn't need environmental cues to reset it daily.
Too short. This would mean your body clock runs ahead of the day, not behind.
Still too short. Your body clock actually runs slightly longer than 24 hours.
Close in direction, but that's too far off — you'd drift almost an hour per day.
Early research suggested this, but it was based on flawed experiments. Modern research shows it's closer to 24 hours.
Way too long. Your body would be massively out of sync within days.
While sleep patterns change with age, the circadian period length is remarkably consistent.

choices.text: It varies by more than 2 hours between people
choices.hint: Individual variation exists, but the average is well-established.

choices.text: It cannot be measured without sunlight
choices.hint: In fact, the most accurate measurements were made deep underground, completely without sunlight.

correctExplanation: Your internal clock runs on a cycle of about 24 hours and 15 minutes, not exactly 24 hours. This means without environmental cues like sunlight, your sleep schedule would drift later by about 15 minutes each day. This is why consistent morning light exposure is so important — it resets your slightly-too-long clock back to 24 hours every day.

text: Dr. Matthew Walker's research at UC Berkeley found that pulling an all-nighter before learning new information results in what level of deficit in the ability to make new memories, compared to a full night of sleep?

choices.hint: Far too mild. Sleep deprivation has a dramatic impact on memory formation.
Still too low. The brain's memory circuits are severely compromised without sleep.
Getting closer, but the real impact is far worse than most people expect.
This is the benefit a nap gives you. An all-nighter costs far more.
Not quite. The hippocampus is even more disrupted than this suggests.
Close, but slightly overestimated. Though the real number is shocking enough.
Too high, though the actual number is still devastating.
This would mean almost no learning at all. The real deficit is serious but not quite this extreme.

choices.text: It depends entirely on the type of information
choices.hint: While the type matters somewhat, the deficit is consistent across many types of learning.

correctExplanation: A single all-nighter produces a 40% deficit in your ability to make new memories. Your hippocampus — the brain's memory inbox — effectively shuts its doors when you're sleep-deprived. That's the difference between acing an exam and failing it, between remembering someone's name and forgetting it instantly.

text: Brain imaging studies showed that sleep-deprived individuals had a dramatically amplified response in their amygdala (the brain's emotional center) when shown disturbing images. How much greater was the emotional reaction compared to well-rested participants?

choices.hint: Far too low. Sleep deprivation profoundly destabilizes emotional regulation.
Still too conservative. The amygdala's response is dramatically heightened.
Getting warmer, but the real amplification is even more extreme.
Close, but the brain's emotional center is even more unhinged than this.
Too high, though the actual amplification is deeply concerning.
Not quite doubled, but the actual number is still alarming.
This would mean a tripled response. The real effect is large but not this extreme.

choices.text: It varies too much to quantify
choices.hint: Brain scanning has allowed precise measurement of this effect.

choices.text: The amygdala actually shows less activity
choices.hint: The opposite is true — sleep deprivation supercharges the amygdala.

correctExplanation: Sleep-deprived brains showed a 60% amplification in amygdala reactivity. Without sleep, the prefrontal cortex — your rational brake pedal — loses its connection to the emotional gas pedal. You're not just a little more emotional when tired; you're operating with a 60% more reactive emotional brain and without the rational control to manage it.

text: Sleep has evolved independently in every animal species ever studied. Among mammals, which animal sleeps approximately 19 hours per day — the most of any mammal?

choices.hint: Elephants sleep only about 4 hours per day — among the least of any mammal.
Despite their reputation, sloths sleep about 10 hours per day in the wild.
Cats sleep about 12–16 hours, but there's a mammal that sleeps even more.
Koalas sleep about 14–16 hours — a lot, but not the record holder.
Lions sleep about 15 hours per day. Close, but not the champion sleeper.
Opossums sleep about 18 hours — close to the top but not quite.
Armadillos are heavy sleepers at about 16–18 hours, but one mammal beats them.
Hamsters sleep about 14 hours per day — less than several other mammals.

choices.text: All mammals sleep roughly the same amount
choices.hint: Sleep duration varies enormously across mammals, from 4 to 19 hours per day.

correctExplanation: The brown bat sleeps approximately 19 hours per day, making it the longest-sleeping mammal. At the other extreme, elephants sleep only about 4 hours. Sleep duration in mammals appears to be influenced by body size, metabolic rate, predation risk, and diet complexity — but the fact that every animal sleeps, despite the enormous vulnerability it creates, tells us sleep provides something so vital that evolution has never been able to eliminate it.

text: According to research cited by the World Health Organization, what fraction of adults in developed nations fail to obtain the recommended 8 hours of sleep per night?

choices.hint: Far too optimistic. Sleep insufficiency is far more widespread than this.
Still too low. The sleep crisis affects a much larger portion of the population.
Higher than you'd hope, but the real number is even worse.
Getting closer. The reality is grimmer still.
Very close, but the actual figure is even higher than half.
This would mean 75% are failing — the real number is high but not quite this extreme.
80% would represent almost universal sleep deprivation. The real figure is lower.
Nearly everyone — too extreme. Many people do manage adequate sleep.

choices.text: The data is insufficient to estimate
choices.hint: Extensive surveys across many countries have established this figure quite clearly.

correctExplanation: Two-thirds of adults in developed nations fail to obtain 8 hours of sleep. The WHO has declared a sleep loss epidemic throughout industrialized nations. You are almost certainly one of these people, and you're reading this right now instead of sleeping — which is exactly the problem.

text: Dr. Michael Irwin found that a single night of only 4 hours of sleep swept away what percentage of natural killer cells (your cancer-fighting immune cells) compared to a full 8-hour night?

hints.tooLowClose: You're in the right ballpark, but the immune hit is even worse than that.
hints.tooHighClose: Close, but the drop isn't quite that catastrophic — though the real number is bad enough.
hints.tooLowFar: Way too low. Sleep deprivation obliterates your immune frontline far more dramatically than most people realise.
hints.tooHighFar: Too high. Your body does retain some cancer-fighting cells, but not nearly enough to protect you properly.

correctExplanation: A single bad night of just 4 hours stripped away 70% of your natural killer cells. These are the cells that hunt down and destroy cancerous mutations in your body every single day. Now imagine the state of your immune army after a week, a month, or years of short sleep.

text: Dr. Maiken Nedergaard discovered a sewage-like cleaning system in the brain that kicks into high gear during deep NREM sleep, flushing out toxic metabolic waste including Alzheimer's-related amyloid protein. What is this system called?
correctExplanation: The glymphatic system is your brain's night-shift cleaning crew. During deep NREM sleep, glial cells shrink by up to 60%, creating channels for cerebrospinal fluid to power-wash toxic waste from your brain — including the amyloid plaques linked to Alzheimer's. It's a 10–20x increase in cleaning power compared to when you're awake. Skipping sleep is like telling the cleaning crew to go home — the toxic garbage just piles up.

text: Researchers quarantined participants and exposed them to the rhinovirus (common cold). Those sleeping 5 hours or less had an infection rate of roughly 50%. What was the approximate infection rate for those sleeping 7 or more hours?

hints.tooLowClose: Not quite that low. Adequate sleep provides strong protection, but it doesn't make you nearly immune.
hints.tooHighClose: A bit too high. Sleep offers more protection against the common cold than you're giving it credit for.
hints.tooLowFar: Too low. While sleep helps enormously, it doesn't reduce infection rates to near-zero.
hints.tooHighFar: Way too high. The gap between short and adequate sleepers is far more dramatic than that — sleep is a powerful shield.

correctExplanation: Those sleeping 7+ hours had roughly an 18% infection rate versus about 50% for those sleeping 5 hours or less. Nearly three times the protection, just from sleeping adequately. Your immune system doesn't just benefit from sleep — it fundamentally depends on it.

text: Sleep alternates between NREM and REM stages in repeating cycles throughout the night. Each NREM-REM cycle lasts approximately how many minutes?

hints.tooLowClose: A bit too short. Each cycle needs enough time for the brain to complete both NREM and REM stages.
hints.tooHighClose: Slightly too long. At that length you'd get fewer complete cycles per night than research shows.
hints.tooLowFar: Much too short. That wouldn't give the brain enough time to cycle through all sleep stages even once.
hints.tooHighFar: Way too long. At that duration you'd only complete a couple of cycles in a full night of sleep.

correctExplanation: Each sleep cycle lasts about 90 minutes. But here's what matters: the ratio of NREM to REM within each cycle changes across the night. Early cycles are dominated by deep NREM sleep; later cycles are dominated by REM sleep. This means if you cut your sleep short by even 90 minutes, you lose a disproportionate amount of REM sleep — the stage critical for emotional regulation and creativity.

text: In a flu vaccine study, participants who were restricted to 4 hours of sleep per night in the week before vaccination produced less than what percentage of the normal antibody response compared to well-rested participants?

hints.tooLowClose: A bit too low. Sleep deprivation cripples the immune response, but the body holds on to slightly more than that.
hints.tooHighClose: Slightly too high. The reduction in antibody production is even more alarming than your guess.
hints.tooLowFar: Much too low. The immune system is weakened by sleep loss, but vaccine response doesn't drop that drastically.
hints.tooHighFar: Way too high. Sleep deprivation's impact on vaccine effectiveness is far worse than you think — your body barely registers the vaccine.

correctExplanation: Sleep-restricted participants produced less than 50% of the normal antibody response to the flu vaccine. In other words, their bodies barely recognized the vaccine. Sleep is so fundamental to immune function that without it, even medical interventions designed to protect you are rendered largely ineffective.

text: You drink a cup of coffee containing {caffeine_mg}mg of caffeine at {drink_time}. Caffeine's half-life is {half_life} hours. How many milligrams of caffeine are still circulating in your brain at {check_time}?
correctExplanation: Even hours after your last sip, caffeine is still fighting sleep in your brain. At {check_time}, you'd still have {answer}mg of caffeine active — that's like drinking {ROUND(DIVIDE(answer, 95), 1)} of a fresh cup right as you're trying to sleep. Your adenosine receptors are being blocked, your brain can't hear the 'time to sleep' signal, and you're paying for it with lighter, more fragmented sleep.

text: A healthy person normally has approximately {nk_cells} natural killer cells per unit of blood. After a single night of only 4 hours of sleep, they lose 70% of those cells. How many natural killer cells remain?
correctExplanation: From {nk_cells} cells, only {answer} remain after just one night of 4-hour sleep — that's a 70% wipeout of your frontline cancer defense. These natural killer cells are responsible for identifying and eliminating cancerous mutations that occur in your body every day. One bad night is all it takes to leave your body virtually undefended.

text: A student memorizes {word_count} vocabulary words before bed. Research shows sleep provides a 20-40% memory retention benefit compared to the same time spent awake. Assuming a {retention_pct}% retention benefit from sleep, how many additional words would the sleeping student retain compared to the student who stayed awake?
correctExplanation: The sleeping student retains {answer} more words — a {retention_pct}% advantage, just from sleeping. Sleep doesn't just prevent forgetting; it actively strengthens memories. During deep NREM sleep, your brain replays and consolidates the day's learning, moving memories from temporary storage in the hippocampus to permanent storage in the cortex.

text: A European study of 25,000 people found that sleeping 6 hours or less increases cancer risk by 40% compared to sleeping 7+ hours. If a population of {population} people all slept 7+ hours, approximately {base_rate} would develop cancer. How many additional cancer cases would you expect if they all slept 6 hours or less instead?
correctExplanation: That's {answer} additional cancer cases — a 40% increase — driven entirely by insufficient sleep. The World Health Organization has classified nighttime shift work as a 'probable carcinogen.' You don't need to work night shifts to damage yourself; routinely sleeping 6 hours or less produces the same biological assault on your body.

text: After just one week of sleeping 6 hours per night, researchers found that the activity of a staggering number of genes was distorted — roughly half were up-regulated (inflammation, stress) and half were down-regulated (immune function, metabolism). Approximately how many genes were affected?

hints.tooLowClose: Not quite enough. The genetic disruption from just one week of short sleep is broader than you'd expect.
hints.tooHighClose: Close, but a bit too many. The real number is staggering enough without inflating it.
hints.tooLowFar: Far too few. Sleep deprivation wreaks havoc across a much wider portion of your genome than that.
hints.tooHighFar: Too many. While the real number is shockingly high, the human genome doesn't suffer quite that broadly from one week of short sleep.

correctExplanation: 711 genes had their activity distorted after just one week of 6-hour sleep. Half of those genes were cranked up — promoting inflammation, stress responses, and cardiovascular disease. The other half were dialed down — suppressing immune function and metabolic regulation. Your DNA literally changes its behavior when you don't sleep enough.
